seoTitle: Set Up Your Check Printer in QuickBooks Online
metaDescription: Learn how to configure and align your printer with QuickBooks Online's check printing tools so voucher and standard checks come out perfectly formatted.
intro: If you print checks straight from QuickBooks Online, a few minutes spent on printer setup saves you from wasted check stock later. This guide walks through connecting your printer, running an alignment test, and fine-tuning margins so your payee, date, and amount fields land exactly where they belong on pre-printed check stock.

steps.heading: Open the Print Checks screen
steps.body: From the + New menu, choose Print Checks under the Vendors column to reach the print queue where setup begins.

steps.heading: Launch Print Setup
steps.body: Click Print Setup to choose whether you're using Voucher or Standard check stock, matching the physical checks you have on hand.

steps.heading: Run an alignment test
steps.body: Use the preview and print sample option to generate a test layout, then hold it against a real blank check to check for drift in the text positions.

steps.heading: Fix your printer's scaling
steps.body: In your browser's print dialog, set page scaling to Actual Size instead of Fit to Page, since scaling distorts where fields land on the check.

steps.heading: Adjust and lock in the layout
steps.body: If lines are off, use QuickBooks' adjustment fields to nudge text horizontally or vertically, then save the configuration once the test print lines up cleanly.

faq.question: Why does my printed check look shifted even though the preview looked fine?
faq.answer: This is usually a browser scaling issue — make sure your print dialog is set to Actual Size rather than Fit to Page, which stretches or shrinks the layout.

faq.question: Do I need to redo alignment every time I print?
faq.answer: No, once you save your alignment settings they're stored for that printer profile, so future check runs use the same calibration.

faq.question: Can I use both Voucher and Standard check styles?
faq.answer: Yes, but each style has its own layout, so you'll want to set up and save alignment separately for whichever stock you switch to.

faq.question: What should I do if the test print still doesn't line up after adjusting?
faq.answer: Try printing another test sample after each adjustment rather than guessing at multiple changes at once, since small offsets compound quickly.
